description: Side-by-side comparison of CNN-based CAM activation maps versus GETAM (transformer-based) activation maps on multiple object images, showing that CAM highlights only discriminative regions while GETAM captures full object shapes.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the core limitation of CNN-based CAMs (partial activation / over-smoothing) versus the improved coverage of transformer-based GETAM, motivating the research problem immediately.
when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on weakly-supervised segmentation to motivate why transformer-based attention maps are preferable to CNN CAMs.
suggested_caption: CAM vs. GETAM: transformers capture full object shapes, not just discriminative regions.

description: Comparison of three CAM variants applied to ViT: CAM (add) which adds the [class] token to patch tokens, CAM (ignore) which ignores the [class] token, and CNN baseline CAM (ResNet38), showing that naive transplantation of CNN CAM to ViT produces poor results.
teaching_value: Illustrates why CNN-based CAM techniques cannot be directly applied to Vision Transformers, highlighting the architectural differences that necessitate a new approach like GETAM.
when_to_show: Show when explaining why a new method is needed for ViT-based segmentation, after introducing the CAM concept and before presenting GETAM.
suggested_caption: Naive CAM transplantation to ViT fails; a new approach is required.

description: Grid of visualizations showing class-agnostic attention maps (leftmost column) and class-wise gradient maps (rightmost column) for multiple images, demonstrating that gradient maps retain clear object-specific shapes while attention maps show broader candidate regions.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the complementary roles of attention maps and gradient maps in GETAM: attention provides recall, gradients provide class-specific precision.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the two components of GETAM (attention map and gradient map) to illustrate what each component contributes before explaining their combination.
suggested_caption: Class-agnostic attention (left) vs. class-wise gradient maps (right) in GETAM.

description: Diagram of GETAM generation within a single transformer block, showing how the class-agnostic attention map (A_CLS^i) and the class-wise gradient map (∇A_CLS) are computed and combined via element-wise multiplication to produce the GETAM output.
teaching_value: Provides a concrete architectural diagram of the GETAM computation pipeline, enabling students to understand the data flow and mathematical operations (element-wise multiplication, ReLU, gradient computation) within a transformer block.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the GETAM formulation in detail, as the primary architectural reference diagram for the method.
suggested_caption: GETAM combines class-agnostic attention and gradient maps inside each transformer block.

description: Grid of attention maps from successive transformer blocks for two sample images (one with multiple people), showing how different layers focus on different object regions (foreground vs. background people) without low-level noise.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the benefit of aggregating attention across successive transformer layers, showing that different layers capture complementary object parts, motivating the successive attention aggregation strategy.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the successive attention aggregation component of GETAM, to justify why multiple transformer blocks are combined rather than using a single layer.
suggested_caption: Successive transformer blocks focus on different object regions, enabling better coverage.

description: Distribution plots comparing class-wise attention maps produced by element-wise multiplication versus matrix multiplication (dot product) fusion methods, showing that element-wise multiplication concentrates the distribution while matrix multiplication introduces noise.
teaching_value: Provides empirical justification for the design choice of element-wise multiplication in GETAM over matrix multiplication, helping students understand the trade-off between concentration and noise in attention fusion.
when_to_show: Show during the ablation study discussion or when justifying the element-wise multiplication design choice in GETAM.
suggested_caption: Element-wise multiplication concentrates attention; matrix multiplication introduces noise.

description: Three-panel diagram illustrating the activation-aware label completion module: (a) all objects located via activation maps, (b) saliency constraint providing accurate shapes for salient objects but suppressing non-salient ones, (c) high activation mining recovering suppressed background objects.
teaching_value: Visually explains the two-stage pseudo-label refinement pipeline (saliency constrained masking + high activation mining), showing how the two components address complementary failure cases.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the label refinement pipeline after GETAM, to illustrate how pseudo labels are improved for both salient and non-salient objects.
suggested_caption: Label completion: saliency masking for salient objects, activation mining for non-salient ones.

description: Conceptual comparison diagram showing (a) multi-step WSSS methods requiring multiple stages and networks versus (b) the proposed single-stage transformer approach trainable end-to-end in one pass.
teaching_value: Clearly contrasts the architectural complexity of multi-stage WSSS pipelines with the proposed end-to-end single-stage approach, helping students appreciate the efficiency advantage of the transformer-based method.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the overall framework to contrast it with prior multi-stage methods and motivate the single-stage design.
suggested_caption: Multi-step WSSS (a) vs. proposed single-stage transformer framework (b).

description: Qualitative comparison grid showing activation maps from four methods on the same images: (a) original image, (b) CNN-based CAM, (c) ViT-based Grad-CAM, (d) GETAM, demonstrating GETAM's superior object shape capture and noise suppression.
teaching_value: Provides direct visual evidence of GETAM's advantage over both CNN-based CAM and ViT-based Grad-CAM, helping students evaluate the method's effectiveness on real examples.
when_to_show: Show during the experimental results section to support quantitative claims with qualitative visual evidence of GETAM's superiority.
suggested_caption: GETAM (d) captures better object shapes than CNN CAM (b) or ViT Grad-CAM (c).

description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on the nose, other ear, and eyes — illustrating selective visual attention at varying resolutions.
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, concrete analogy for how attention mechanisms work: humans focus on high-resolution regions (yellow box) while perceiving surroundings at lower resolution, mirroring how neural attention assigns different weights to different input positions.
when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing attention mechanisms, before any math, to ground the abstract concept in human perceptual experience.
suggested_caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus (yellow) with low-res periphery (red).

description: A sentence with attention weights visualized as colored arcs or highlights showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths.
teaching_value: Bridges the visual attention analogy to linguistic attention, showing students that words in a sentence have differential relevance to each other — directly motivating self-attention and cross-attention in NLP models.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention (Shiba Inu) example, when transitioning from image-based intuition to language-based attention motivation.
suggested_caption: Words attend to other words with varying weights in a sentence.

description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an encoder RNN processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder RNN then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates the bottleneck problem of seq2seq models: all source information must be compressed into a single fixed-length vector, making it clear why long sentences are problematic and why attention is needed.
when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, just before introducing the attention mechanism as a solution.
suggested_caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one fixed vector.

description: Diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder architecture, showing alignment scores computed between decoder hidden states and all encoder hidden states, with weighted context vectors fed into the decoder at each time step.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how attention solves the bottleneck: instead of one fixed vector, the decoder dynamically attends to all encoder states via learned alignment weights, making the information flow explicit and traceable.
when_to_show: Show when formally introducing the Bahdanau (2015) attention mechanism, after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem.
suggested_caption: Bahdanau additive attention: decoder dynamically attends to all encoder states.

description: A heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates the attention weight between each source-target word pair, revealing mostly diagonal alignment with some cross-language reordering.
teaching_value: Provides empirical evidence that learned attention weights correspond to meaningful linguistic alignments, including non-monotonic reorderings between languages, making the abstract alignment score concept tangible and interpretable.
when_to_show: Show after explaining alignment scores mathematically, to demonstrate that the model learns linguistically meaningful alignments without explicit supervision.
suggested_caption: Alignment matrix: French-English attention weights reveal word correspondences. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)

description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red, and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing which prior words the model attends to.
teaching_value: Demonstrates self-attention in action within a single sequence, showing students how a model can capture long-range dependencies by attending to relevant earlier words, making the concept of intra-attention concrete.
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention, to illustrate how a model relates different positions within the same sequence.
suggested_caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior words with varying strength. (Cheng et al., 2016)

description: An image of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park with overlaid attention weight regions highlighted, showing which parts of the image the model attends to when generating each word of the caption 'A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park.'
teaching_value: Illustrates soft attention applied to image captioning, showing students how attention weights over spatial image regions correspond to semantically relevant objects for each generated word, making soft attention interpretable.
when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs hard attention, or when explaining attention applied to non-sequential (spatial) data like images.
suggested_caption: Soft attention highlights relevant image regions per caption word. (Xu et al., 2015)

description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in the Luong et al. architecture.
teaching_value: Clearly contrasts two attention strategies: global (soft-like, computationally expensive but complete) vs local (window-based, more efficient and differentiable), helping students understand the trade-offs in attention design.
when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. (2015) attention variants, after covering Bahdanau attention, to illustrate the spectrum from soft to hard attention.
suggested_caption: Global vs local attention: full-sequence vs windowed source alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)

description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine showing a controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.
teaching_value: Shows how attention mechanisms extend beyond sequence-to-sequence tasks to external memory access, demonstrating that attention is a general differentiable addressing mechanism — connecting NTM to the broader attention framework.
when_to_show: Show when introducing Neural Turing Machines as an application of attention to memory access, after students understand basic attention in seq2seq models.
suggested_caption: NTM architecture: controller uses attention to read/write external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.
teaching_value: Helps students understand the design choice of how to parameterize shift distributions in NTM location-based addressing, making the tradeoff between approaches concrete and visual.
when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing and how the controller produces a distribution over allowable integer shifts; use to prompt discussion of why different parameterizations matter.
suggested_caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.

description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.
teaching_value: Provides a complete end-to-end visual of how NTM attention is computed step-by-step, allowing students to trace each mathematical operation through the pipeline and understand how content and location addressing combine.
when_to_show: After introducing each individual NTM addressing component; use as a synthesis diagram to show how all steps connect into a unified addressing pipeline.
suggested_caption: NTM addressing flow: content lookup, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)

description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.
teaching_value: Visually distinguishes Pointer Networks from standard seq-2-seq by showing that the output is a pointer into the input, helping students grasp how attention scores are repurposed as output probabilities over variable-length inputs.
when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems with variable output spaces (e.g., sorting, TSP); contrast with standard attention to highlight the key architectural difference.
suggested_caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions as output. (Vinyals et al., 2015)

description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, scaling, optional masking, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, concatenation, and linear projection.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how multi-head attention extends single-head attention by running multiple attention operations in parallel and combining results, helping students understand both the mechanics and the motivation for attending to multiple representation subspaces.
when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer architecture; introduce single-head attention first, then use this diagram to show how multi-head attention generalizes it.
suggested_caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing a multi-head self-attention sublayer and a position-wise feed-forward sublayer, both with residual connections and layer normalization.
teaching_value: Shows students the modular structure of the Transformer encoder, making explicit how positional encoding, self-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, and layer norm compose into a single encoder block that is stacked N times.
when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder in detail; use to walk through each component layer by layer before discussing the decoder.
suggested_caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, and feed-forward sublayers, all with residual connections and layer norm, ending in a linear + softmax output layer.
teaching_value: Highlights the key differences between encoder and decoder: the masked self-attention (preventing future token leakage) and the cross-attention over encoder outputs, helping students understand autoregressive generation and how the decoder retrieves encoded information.
when_to_show: After explaining the encoder; use to contrast encoder and decoder structures, emphasizing masking and cross-attention as the key additions.
suggested_caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and encoder cross-attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Complete Transformer model architecture diagram showing the full encoder stack on the left and decoder stack on the right, with the cross-attention connection between them, input/output embeddings, positional encodings, and the final linear+softmax output layer.
teaching_value: Provides a unified view of the entire Transformer, allowing students to see how encoder and decoder interact and trace the full data flow from input tokens to output probabilities in one diagram.
when_to_show: As a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately; use for review or to answer questions about how the full model fits together.
suggested_caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)

description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and causal attention layers (for selective long-range access), used for meta-learning tasks.
teaching_value: Illustrates how SNAIL combines causal convolutions and attention to address the Transformer's weakness with positional dependencies, helping students understand hybrid architectures that leverage both local structure and global attention.
when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer regarding positional sensitivity and how SNAIL addresses them; useful for comparing attention-only vs. hybrid sequential models.
suggested_caption: SNAIL interleaves causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)

description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the limited local receptive field of a convolutional operation (small highlighted region around a pixel) versus the global receptive field of self-attention (connections from one pixel to all other positions in the image).
teaching_value: Intuitively demonstrates why self-attention is more powerful than convolution for capturing long-range spatial dependencies, motivating the use of self-attention in GANs and other vision models.
when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or self-attention in vision; use to motivate why convolution alone is insufficient for modeling global image structure.
suggested_caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention accesses all positions globally.

description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), the attention map computation via softmax, and the final weighted output combined with the original feature map via a learned scale parameter.
teaching_value: Concretely maps the abstract query-key-value attention framework onto a convolutional image setting, helping students understand how Transformer-style attention is adapted for spatial feature maps in GANs.
when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
suggested_caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features as query, key, value via 1×1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.
teaching_value: Demonstrates the practical output quality of SAGAN, showing students how self-attention mechanisms in GANs enable the model to capture long-range spatial dependencies and generate globally consistent, high-resolution images — something local convolutions alone struggle to achieve.
when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — use it to ask 'which parts of the image do you think the attention map focuses on for a dog's eye?'
suggested_caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)

description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, styled as an 'opened' black box
teaching_value: Gives students a first structural decomposition of the Transformer, establishing the encoder-decoder paradigm before diving into details
when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer lesson, immediately after introducing the model as a black box, to motivate the internal structure
suggested_caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with an input sentence in one language entering and a translated sentence exiting
teaching_value: Anchors the abstract architecture in a concrete use case (machine translation), helping students understand the input-output contract before examining internals
when_to_show: Show at the very beginning of a Transformer introduction to ground the architecture in a real-world task before opening the black box
suggested_caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translation out. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing a stack of six encoders on the left and a stack of six decoders on the right, with arrows indicating data flow and connections between the two stacks
teaching_value: Clarifies that both encoder and decoder are stacks of repeated identical-structure layers, and that the number six is a design choice, not a fixed rule
when_to_show: Show after introducing the black-box view, when explaining that the Transformer is composed of repeated encoder and decoder layers
suggested_caption: Six stacked encoders feed into six stacked decoders in the Transformer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with input flowing up through both
teaching_value: Isolates the internal structure of one encoder, making the two-sub-layer composition concrete and showing the sequential data flow from self-attention to feed-forward
when_to_show: Show when drilling into a single encoder block after establishing the stack structure, to explain what each encoder actually computes
suggested_caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar)

description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and Feed-Forward Neural Network, with inputs from both the encoder stack and the previous decoder output
teaching_value: Shows how the decoder differs from the encoder by adding a cross-attention layer that attends to encoder outputs, making the encoder-decoder connection explicit
when_to_show: Show after explaining the encoder structure, when contrasting the decoder to highlight the additional cross-attention mechanism
suggested_caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing input words being converted into embedding vectors (represented as colored columns of size 512) before entering the bottom encoder, with each word mapped to a fixed-size vector
teaching_value: Illustrates the word-to-vector conversion step and establishes the 512-dimensional embedding size as the standard input representation throughout the encoder stack
when_to_show: Show when transitioning from architecture overview to data flow, specifically when explaining how raw words become numerical inputs to the Transformer
suggested_caption: Input words converted to 512-dimensional embedding vectors before encoding. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors flowing in parallel through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers of an encoder, with each word following its own path
teaching_value: Visualizes the parallel processing property of the Transformer encoder and shows that each token has its own path, while hinting at inter-path dependencies in self-attention
when_to_show: Show when explaining how the encoder processes a full sequence, emphasizing parallelism as a key advantage over RNNs
suggested_caption: Each word vector flows its own path through encoder layers in parallel. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating the encoder processing a short example sentence, showing how each token's vector representation passes through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers with concrete word labels
teaching_value: Makes the abstract data-flow diagram concrete by attaching real words to the vectors, helping students trace how a specific sentence is encoded step by step
when_to_show: Show when walking through a worked example of encoding, after introducing the encoder structure, to connect theory to a concrete sentence
suggested_caption: Concrete sentence tokens flowing through encoder self-attention and feed-forward layers. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization heatmap showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending strongly to 'animal', with colored lines or shading indicating attention weights between the query word and all other words
teaching_value: Provides an intuitive, visual proof of what self-attention computes — that a pronoun can learn to refer back to its antecedent — making the abstract mechanism tangible
when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention conceptually, before the mathematical details, to motivate why attention is useful for capturing word relationships
suggested_caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', visualizing learned word relationships. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors are derived from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, with vector dimensions labeled (64 vs 512)
teaching_value: Demystifies the Q/K/V abstraction by showing the exact linear transformation that produces each vector, and explains the dimensionality reduction from 512 to 64 as an architectural choice
when_to_show: Show when explaining the first step of self-attention computation, after motivating why attention is useful, to ground the Q/K/V formalism in concrete matrix operations
suggested_caption: Query, Key, Value vectors derived from embeddings via learned weight matrices. (Alammar)

description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates the dot-product scoring mechanism, showing students exactly which vectors interact and why — making the abstract formula q·k tangible with labeled arrows and example words.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
suggested_caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)

description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.
teaching_value: Visually connects three sequential steps — scaling, softmax normalization, and value weighting — helping students understand why scaling stabilizes gradients and how softmax converts raw scores into interpretable attention weights.
when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
suggested_caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.
teaching_value: Demonstrates how the weighted sum aggregates information from all positions into a single output vector, making the 'soft lookup' intuition concrete — high-attention words contribute more to the output.
when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
suggested_caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)

description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.
teaching_value: Bridges the word-level intuition to the efficient matrix implementation, showing students how batching all positions into a single matrix multiplication replaces the per-word vector operations described earlier.
when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
suggested_caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)

description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.
teaching_value: Presents the canonical self-attention formula in visual form, helping students connect the step-by-step process to the compact mathematical expression used in the original paper.
when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
suggested_caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)

description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same input being processed through eight parallel sets of Q/K/V weight matrices, each producing a separate Z matrix (Z0 through Z7).
teaching_value: Makes the parallelism of multi-head attention visually explicit, helping students understand that multiple independent attention computations run simultaneously, each potentially capturing different relationships.
when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
suggested_caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)

description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.
teaching_value: Motivates the need for the concatenation and projection step by making the dimensionality mismatch problem concrete — students see why eight matrices must be combined before passing to the next layer.
when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head QKV diagram to pose the 'how do we combine these?' problem before revealing the W^O projection solution.
suggested_caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)

description: Diagram showing the concatenation of all eight Z matrices followed by multiplication by weight matrix W^O to produce a single combined output matrix, completing the multi-headed attention mechanism.
teaching_value: Shows the concrete solution to combining multiple attention heads — concatenation followed by a learned linear projection — teaching students how the model learns to integrate information from all representation subspaces.
when_to_show: Show after presenting the eight Z matrices problem, as the resolution step, and emphasize that W^O is also a learned parameter trained end-to-end.
suggested_caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)

description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-headed self-attention pipeline in one view: input embeddings → multiple Q/K/V projections → parallel attention computations → concatenation → W^O projection → final output.
teaching_value: Provides a unified visual summary of all multi-head attention components together, helping students consolidate the full pipeline and see how all the pieces (weight matrices, heads, projections) fit together.
when_to_show: Show as a consolidation diagram after explaining each individual step, or use as a reference when students need to review the complete multi-head attention mechanism.
suggested_caption: Complete multi-headed self-attention pipeline from input to output. (Alammar)

description: Attention visualization showing which tokens different attention heads focus on when encoding the word 'it' in the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to other words.
teaching_value: Provides an interpretability example showing that different attention heads learn to resolve different linguistic relationships (e.g., coreference), making the abstract concept of 'representation subspaces' concrete and meaningful.
when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-headed attention mechanics to demonstrate real-world utility — use it to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial by showing they capture different linguistic phenomena.
suggested_caption: Attention heads reveal which words 'it' attends to during encoding. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, creating a complex, hard-to-interpret web of attention weights
teaching_value: Demonstrates why multi-head attention is powerful but visually complex — students see that different heads attend to different parts of the sequence, and understand the interpretability challenge when all heads are shown at once
when_to_show: After explaining individual attention heads; use to motivate why we need multiple heads and to discuss the interpretability trade-off of richer representations
suggested_caption: All attention heads for 'it' — multi-head attention visualized. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder, with a small 4-dimensional example illustrating the sinusoidal pattern of positional encoding values
teaching_value: Concretely shows students how positional information is injected into the model by adding a fixed vector to each embedding, making abstract positional encoding tangible with a low-dimensional example
when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use the 4D example to build intuition before showing the full 512-dimensional version
suggested_caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar, 2018)

description: Small color-coded grid showing actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding across several word positions, with values ranging from -1 to 1 displayed as colored cells
teaching_value: Gives students a concrete, readable example of what positional encoding values look like numerically, bridging the gap between the formula and the actual numbers added to embeddings
when_to_show: Immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding; use as a stepping stone before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap
suggested_caption: Actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Large color-coded heatmap grid where each row represents the positional encoding vector for one word position (up to ~20 positions) and each column represents one of 512 dimensions, with colors ranging from dark to light showing sinusoidal patterns across dimensions
teaching_value: Reveals the sinusoidal structure of positional encodings at scale — students can see how the pattern changes across positions and dimensions, and understand why this encoding generalizes to unseen sequence lengths
when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss the property of generalizing to longer sequences than seen during training
suggested_caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap across word positions. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Reproduction of the positional encoding visualization from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sinusoidal pattern across positions and dimensions as a color-coded heatmap
teaching_value: Connects the blog's explanation directly to the original paper's figure, helping students recognize the canonical representation and understand the slight difference between the paper's interleaved method and the Tensor2Tensor implementation
concepts: Attention Is All You Need paper
when_to_show: When discussing the difference between the paper's positional encoding method and the Tensor2Tensor implementation; use to teach students to read primary sources alongside blog explanations
suggested_caption: Positional encoding from 'Attention Is All You Need' paper (interleaved). (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection around the self-attention sub-layer and the subsequent layer normalization step, with vectors flowing through Add & Norm operations
teaching_value: Visually explains the residual connection and layer normalization pattern that stabilizes training — students see exactly where these operations occur relative to the self-attention computation
when_to_show: When introducing residual connections and layer norm in the encoder; use to show the precise placement of Add & Norm before discussing why these are needed for deep network training
suggested_caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around self-attention sub-layer. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Architecture diagram of a Transformer with 2 stacked encoders and 2 stacked decoders, showing residual connections and layer normalization at each sub-layer in both encoder and decoder stacks
teaching_value: Shows the full stacked architecture with residual connections throughout, helping students understand how the pattern repeats across all layers and both encoder and decoder sides
when_to_show: After explaining residual connections in a single layer; use to show how the pattern scales across the full model and to transition from encoder-only to encoder-decoder discussion
suggested_caption: 2-layer Transformer with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Detailed vector-level diagram of the self-attention sub-layer with residual connection, showing input vectors being processed by self-attention, then added back to the original input vectors (residual), and passed through layer normalization
teaching_value: Makes the residual connection mathematically concrete — students see that the original input vector is literally added to the self-attention output before normalization, clarifying the 'Add' in 'Add & Norm'
when_to_show: When students need a deeper understanding of how residual connections work mechanically; use to show the actual vector arithmetic before moving to the decoder
suggested_caption: Vector-level view of residual addition and layer norm after self-attention. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer: the encoder processes the full input sequence, and the decoder begins generating the first output token, with attention vectors K and V flowing from the top encoder to each decoder layer
teaching_value: Animates the encoder-to-decoder information flow, showing students how encoder outputs (K, V vectors) are passed to the decoder's encoder-decoder attention layer and how decoding begins autoregressively
when_to_show: When introducing the decoder side; use the animation to show the first decoding step and explain how encoder context is passed to the decoder
suggested_caption: First decoding step: encoder outputs K, V vectors fed to decoder. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, with each generated token being fed back as input to the decoder in the next step, illustrating the autoregressive generation process until an end token is produced
teaching_value: Illustrates the full autoregressive decoding loop — students see how each output token becomes the next input, and how the decoder attends to both previous outputs (masked self-attention) and encoder outputs across multiple steps
when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use to show the complete generation process and explain masked self-attention (attending only to previous positions) in the decoder
suggested_caption: Autoregressive decoding: each output token fed back as next input. (Alammar, 2018)

description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.
teaching_value: Concretely illustrates how raw decoder output (logits) is mapped to a probability distribution over vocabulary and how a single word is selected, bridging abstract math to the actual word-generation mechanism.
when_to_show: Show when explaining how a Transformer generates a token at inference time, specifically after covering the decoder stack and before discussing training loss.
suggested_caption: Linear layer produces logits; softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)

description: Visualization of one-hot encoding for a vocabulary, showing a vector where a single cell is set to 1 (representing the word 'am') and all other cells are 0, against the backdrop of the full output vocabulary.
teaching_value: Makes the abstract concept of one-hot encoding tangible by showing exactly how a single word is represented as a sparse binary vector, which is essential for understanding target labels during training.
when_to_show: Show when introducing how target words are represented during training, before explaining cross-entropy loss or comparing model output distributions to ground truth.
suggested_caption: One-hot encoding represents word 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)

description: Example one-hot encoded vector for a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), illustrating how the word 'thanks' would be represented as a target label when training on the translation of 'merci'.
teaching_value: Provides a minimal, concrete example of one-hot encoding with a small vocabulary, making it easy for students to see the full vector and understand how ground-truth labels are structured before scaling to real vocabulary sizes.
when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing one-hot encoding during the training recap, especially when walking through the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
suggested_caption: Six-word vocabulary: 'thanks' encoded as a one-hot vector. (Alammar)

description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the target one-hot label vector for the word 'thanks', illustrating the discrepancy the loss function must minimize.
teaching_value: Visually demonstrates what the loss function is comparing — a spread probability distribution from an untrained model versus a sharp one-hot target — making cross-entropy loss intuitive and motivating the need for training.
when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function, specifically to illustrate why we need a metric to compare model output distributions to ground-truth labels.
suggested_caption: Model output vs. target label: the gap the loss function minimizes. (Alammar)

description: Grid of probability distributions showing what the model should output at each decoding step for the translation 'i am a student <eos>', with each column representing a time step and each row a vocabulary word, showing ideal peaked distributions at the correct words.
teaching_value: Illustrates the sequential nature of Transformer decoding and the training objective across multiple time steps, helping students understand that the model must learn to produce correct distributions at every position, not just one.
when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step sequence generation during training, after the single-word loss example, to generalize the loss concept to full sentence translation.
suggested_caption: Target probability distributions across all decoding steps for 'i am a student'. (Alammar)

description: Grid of probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model for the translation 'i am a student <eos>', showing sharply peaked distributions near the correct words at each time step, contrasting with the flat distributions of an untrained model.
teaching_value: Shows students what a successfully trained model's output looks like — confident, peaked distributions — providing a concrete goal for training and illustrating the effect of optimization on the model's probability estimates.
when_to_show: Show after displaying the target distributions (image 5) to contrast ideal training targets with actual trained-model outputs, and when introducing greedy vs. beam search decoding strategies.
suggested_caption: Trained model produces confident peaked distributions at each decoding step. (Alammar)
